fields.Text:
Retroperitoneal mass with macroscopic fat, thick septations, and heterogeneous enhancement. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Liposarcoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q1. Correct; difficulty: hard. Retroperitoneal liposarcoma should be suggested in the presence of a retroperitoneal mass with internal fat density, clearly seen on the CT (yellow arrows), with associated high T2 signal (blue arrow), as well as decreased signal on fat-saturated imaging (white arrow) with signal similar to other areas of macroscopic f

fields.Text:
Third ventricular lesion near the foramen of Monro in a young adult. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Colloid cyst}}
fields.Extra:
Q2. Correct; difficulty: not listed. A colloid cyst is a rare benign lesion arising within the third ventricle.

fields.Text: A colloid cyst classically arises in the {{c1::third ventricle near the foramen of Monro}} and can cause obstructive hydrocephalus.

fields.Text:

{{c1::Brodie abscess}}

1/3

fields.Text: A Brodie abscess is a {{c1::subacute osteomyelitis abscess}} that can appear as a well-circumscribed metaphyseal lytic lesion.

Q3. Correct; difficulty: hard. The radiograph shows a round, well-circumscribed lytic lesion in the distal fibular metaphysis (red arrows).

Extraocular muscle enlargement with tendinous insertion sparing. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Graves ophthalmopathy}}

{{c1::Toxic multinodular goiter}}

fields.Text: Toxic multinodular goiter shows a heterogeneous thyroid scan with {{c1::multiple hot and cold nodules}}.

Diffuse bony sclerosis with rugger-jersey spine and atrophic native kidneys. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Renal osteodystrophy}}

fields.Text: Renal osteodystrophy can produce diffuse skeletal sclerosis and {{c1::rugger-jersey spine}}.

fields.Text:

{{c1::Emphysematous pyelonephritis}}

Q7. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Emphysematous pyelonephritis on CT shows gas within the renal parenchyma, typically affects diabetic and immunocompromised patients, and requires emergent treatment.

fields.Text:

{{c1::Subseptate uterus}}
fields.Extra:
Q8. Correct; difficulty: hard. The image demonstrates a cleft at the fundus (yellow arrow) with the same echotexture as the surrounding myometrium, which does not deform the fundal contour (purple arrow), and partially divides the endometrium (blue arrows).

fields.Text: Mullerian duct anomalies result from abnormal {{c1::Mullerian duct development, fusion, or septal resorption}} during embryogenesis.

Distended mucin-filled appendix with little adjacent inflammation. Most likely diagnosis?

Radioactive package label shown: maximum surface radiation level?

{{c1::0.5 mrem/hr}}
fields.Extra:
Q10. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Radioactive label type I should have a surface radiation level that does not exceed 0.5 mrem/hr and should have no detectable radiation at 1 meter.

fields.Text:
Liver lesion with homogeneous arterial hyperenhancement, central scar, and no washout. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Focal nodular hyperplasia}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q11. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Focal nodular hyperplasia shows characteristic homogeneous arterial phase hyperenhancement with central scar hypoenhancement, followed by isointensity in portal venous phase and delayed central scar enhancement - no washout distinguishes it from malignancy.






Open review Q11

fields.Text: FNH has homogeneous arterial hyperenhancement, becomes isodense on portal venous phase, and may show {{c1::delayed central scar enhancement}}.
fields.Extra:
Q11. Correct; difficulty: moderate. fields.Text: Dose calibrator constancy is commonly tested with {{c1::Cs-137}} because its long half-life makes daily decay correction negligible.
fields.Extra:
Q12. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Cs-137's 30-year half-life makes daily decay corrections unnecessary, allowing pure assessment of dose calibrator stability rather than source decay—the fundamental requirement for detecting instrument drift in daily constancy testing.




Open review Q12

fields.Text:
Smooth thick rod-shaped breast calcifications in a ductal distribution. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Plasma cell mastitis}}
fields.Extra:
Q13. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Plasma cell mastitis appears as smooth, thick rod-shaped calcifications in a ductal distribution and is benign. However, radiologists must carefully evaluate the entire breast for coexistent malignancy, as ductal carcinoma in situ can occur alongside these benign secretory calcifications.




Open review Q13

fields.Text: Plasma cell mastitis causes benign {{c1::smooth thick rod-shaped calcifications in a ductal distribution}}.
fields.Extra:
Q13. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Plasma cell mastitis appears as smooth, thick rod-shaped calcifications in a ductal distribution and is benign. However, radiologists must carefully evaluate the entire breast for coexistent malignancy, as ductal carcinoma in situ can occur alongside these benign secretory calcifications.





Open review Q13

fields.Text: Plasma cell mastitis has {{c1::smooth thick rod-shaped ductal calcifications}}, unlike the finer pleomorphic calcifications typical of DCIS.

However, radiologists must carefully evaluate the entire breast for coexistent malignancy, as ductal carcinoma in situ can occur alongside these benign secretory calcifications.

fields.Text: Critical imaging results require {{c1::direct Level 1 communication}} with the ordering/responding clinician, often within 30-60 minutes by local policy.
fields.Extra:
Q14. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: For all "critical" results, Level 1 communication is mandated and audited. Such communication requires direct contact (not electronic contact) between the radiologist and the requesting or responding clinician or another licensed health care provider responsible for that patient’s care within 30 to 60 minutes.




Open review Q14

fields.Text: Medullary breast carcinoma usually appears as a noncalcified round or oval mass with {{c1::circumscribed or indistinct margins}}.
fields.Extra:
Q15. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Medullary breast carcinoma is typically seen as a circular/oval type mass lesion with ill-defined or circumscribed margins at mammography. There can be varying degrees of lobulation while calcification is usually not a feature.




Open review Q15

fields.Text:

{{c1::Fibromuscular dysplasia}}

Q16. Correct; difficulty: easy. In this case, a variant accessory right renal artery is shown.

fields.Text: Renal artery fibromuscular dysplasia causing resistant hypertension is initially treated with {{c1::angioplasty alone}}.
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: easy. In this case, a variant accessory right renal artery is shown.

{{c1::Arcuate uterus}}

Q17. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: An arcuate uterus has an endometrium indentation <1 cm at the uterine fundus.

fields.Text: An arcuate uterus has a smooth fundal endometrial indentation less than {{c1::1 cm}}.

fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: easy. The Nuclear Regulatory Commission defines minor and major radioactive spills in Appendix N of the NUREG publication.

fields.Text: Post-meningitis communicating hydrocephalus results from impaired CSF absorption at the {{c1::arachnoid granulations}}.
fields.Extra:
Q19. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: One known complication of meningitis is hydrocephalus, which results from impaired CSF absorption by the arachnoid granulations.

fields.Text:
Infant with enlarging head, hydrocephalus, and frond-like intraventricular mass. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Choroid plexus papilloma/carcinoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q20. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Imaging differentiation between choroid plexus papilloma and carcinoma is difficult, requiring histologic confirmation. Carcinomas show parenchymal invasion with peritumoral edema while papillomas remain intraventricular. Extensive parenchymal invasion suggests malignancy.




Open review Q20

fields.Text: Choroid plexus papilloma and carcinoma can be difficult to distinguish on imaging; {{c1::parenchymal invasion and edema}} suggest carcinoma.
fields.Extra:
Q20. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Imaging differentiation between choroid plexus papilloma and carcinoma is difficult, requiring histologic confirmation. Carcinomas show parenchymal invasion with peritumoral edema while papillomas remain intraventricular. Extensive parenchymal invasion suggests malignancy.

fields.Text: A frond-like intraventricular tumor causing hydrocephalus in an infant suggests {{c1::choroid plexus papilloma/carcinoma}}, not ependymoma.
fields.Extra:
Q20. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Imaging differentiation between choroid plexus papilloma and carcinoma is difficult, requiring histologic confirmation. Carcinomas show parenchymal invasion with peritumoral edema while papillomas remain intraventricular. Extensive parenchymal invasion suggests malignancy.





Open review Q20

fields.Text: NRC written notification for a reportable medical event must be submitted within {{c1::15 days}}.
fields.Extra:
Q21. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: A medical event requires reporting when a therapeutic administration deviates from the prescribed dose by ≥20% AND exceeds dose thresholds (0.05 Sv effective dose equivalent, 0.5 Sv to an organ/tissue, or 0.5 Sv shallow dose equivalent to skin). It also applies to wrong patient, wrong route, wrong radiopharmaceutical, wrong treatment mode, or leaking sealed source when dose thresholds are met. Notification requirements: NRC by phone within 1 calendar day, written report within 15 days, and referring physician and patient within 24 hours.




Open review Q21

fields.Text: A reportable NRC medical event requires written notification within {{c1::15 days}}, after phone notification by the next calendar day.
fields.Extra:
Q21. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: A medical event requires reporting when a therapeutic administration deviates from the prescribed dose by ≥20% AND exceeds dose thresholds (0.05 Sv effective dose equivalent, 0.5 Sv to an organ/tissue, or 0.5 Sv shallow dose equivalent to skin). It also applies to wrong patient, wrong route, wrong radiopharmaceutical, wrong treatment mode, or leaking sealed source when dose thresholds are met. Notification requirements: NRC by phone within 1 calendar day, written report within 15 days, and referring physician and patient within 24 hours.

fields.Text: A steep authority gradient can prevent team members from speaking up and threatens {{c1::patient safety}}.
fields.Extra:
Q22. Correct; difficulty: easy. Authority gradient refers to the balance of decision-making power or the steepness of command hierarchy in a given situation.

fields.Text: The ACR MRI safety framework divides MRI sites into {{c1::4 zones}}.
fields.Extra:
Q25. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: The strong magnetic field of MR scanners produces unique safety issues in the imaging environment. The magnetic field is always on. While the patient is a major focus of safety efforts, the same issues apply to technologists, nurses, and physicians working regularly in the MR environment




Open review Q25

fields.Text:
Infant with markedly dilated renal pelvis and normal-caliber ureter. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::UPJ obstruction}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q26. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: UPJ obstruction appears as a dilated renal pelvis with abrupt thinning at the ureteropelvic junction and calyceal dilatation. Parenchymal thinning and dysplasia may be associated. Ultrasound is the first-line examination, but CT provides more detailed evaluation of the ureteropelvic junction. Crossing vessels should be evaluated as a potential causative factor.






Open review Q26

fields.Text: UPJ obstruction appears as {{c1::dilated renal pelvis with abrupt narrowing at the UPJ and normal-caliber ureter}}.

fields.Text: A {{c1::subsolid}} pulmonary nodule is more suspicious for malignancy than a purely solid nodule of similar size.

fields.Text:
CT with acute gastric rotation and antropyloric junction above/anterior to the GE junction. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Mesentero-axial gastric volvulus}}

Q28. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Mesentero-axial gastric volvulus involves stomach rotation along the mesenteroaxial plane perpendicular to the lesser-greater curvature line. The antrum and pylorus rotate upward and anteriorly. The key CT finding is antropyloric junction positioned superior and anterior to the gastroesophageal junction.

{{c1::ATFL tear}}

fields.Text:
Cirrhotic morphology with caudate hypertrophy, nodular contour, and varices. Most likely cause?

{{c1::Alcoholic cirrhosis}}
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Alcoholic cirrhosis on CT shows characteristic morphologic changes including right lobe atrophy with caudate/left lobe hypertrophy, surface nodularity, and portal hypertension findings of splenomegaly, varices, and collateral vessels. CT effectively identifies portosystemic shunts and assesses portal vein patency in decompensated disease.

fields.Text: In the United States, the most common cause of cirrhosis and portal hypertension is {{c1::alcoholic cirrhosis}}.
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Alcoholic cirrhosis on CT shows characteristic morphologic changes including right lobe atrophy with caudate/left lobe hypertrophy, surface nodularity, and portal hypertension findings of splenomegaly, varices, and collateral vessels. CT effectively identifies portosystemic shunts and assesses portal vein patency in decompensated disease.

fields.Text:
Thin-walled cystic visceral mediastinal mass with fluid/proteinaceous contents. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Bronchogenic cyst}}
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Bronchogenic cyst appears as a fluid-attenuation ovoid or round structure with a thin wall in the visceral mediastinum. Internal fluid may show higher attenuation if proteinaceous content, hemorrhage, or debris is present. Wall thickening and internal air suggest superimposed infection.

fields.Text: Bronchogenic cyst is a foregut duplication cyst that usually appears as a {{c1::fluid-attenuation visceral mediastinal mass}}.
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Bronchogenic cyst appears as a fluid-attenuation ovoid or round structure with a thin wall in the visceral mediastinum. fields.Text: Whole-body radiation dose greater than 10 Gy with vomiting/diarrhea within minutes is {{c1::almost universally fatal}}.
fields.Extra:
Q37. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Whole body dose >10 Gy with nausea/vomiting onset within 10 minutes indicates a very poor prognosis. The timing of prodromal symptoms in acute radiation syndrome directly correlates with survival—earlier onset means worse prognosis, with symptom onset <10 minutes representing overwhelming radiation injury most likely incompatible with survival.

{{c1::Varicocele}}
fields.Extra:
Q40. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Varicoceles require upright position with Valsalva maneuver to demonstrate venous diameter greater than 3 mm and reflux of more than 2 seconds duration (not shown here). Varicoceles are associated with male infertility.

fields.Text:
Obstetric US with amniotic bands. What fetal complication is a concern?

{{c1::Congenital amputations}}
fields.Extra:
Q42. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Amniotic band syndrome (ABS) comprises a wide spectrum of abnormalities, all of which result from entrapment of various fetal body parts in a disrupted amnion. Due to the randomness of entrapment, deficits are variable but frequently involve the limbs.

Open review Q43

fields.Text: ACR appropriateness criteria ratings of {{c1::1 to 3}} mean usually not appropriate.
fields.Extra:
Q44. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The ACR appropriateness criteria are based on clinical conditions and associated modifiers. Each clinical condition includes three variants based on patient factors and symptoms. For each variant, each possible imaging modality is rated on a 1 (low) to 9 (high) scale based on the appropriateness of the modality for the variant under discussion.




Open review Q44

fields.Text:
Cardiac MRI with diffuse non-coronary subendocardial/transmural delayed enhancement. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Cardiac amyloidosis}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Cardiac amyloidosis demonstrates characteristic global subendocardial or transmural late gadolinium enhancement in a non-coronary distribution.




Open review Q45

fields.Text: Cardiac amyloidosis shows {{c1::global subendocardial or transmural delayed enhancement}} in a non-coronary distribution.
fields.Extra:

Open review Q45

fields.Text:
Post-traumatic breast oil cysts/dystrophic calcifications. BI-RADS assessment?

{{c1::BI-RADS 2}}
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Post-traumatic lesions with small round, occasionally lucent-centered calcifications represent fat necrosis from blunt trauma - benign finding that appears as oil cysts or dystrophic calcifications but can occasionally mimic malignancy.





Open review Q46

fields.Text: Post-traumatic breast fat necrosis/oil cysts with eggshell calcification are benign and assessed as {{c1::BI-RADS 2}}.
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Post-traumatic lesions with small round, occasionally lucent-centered calcifications represent fat necrosis from blunt trauma - benign finding that appears as oil cysts or dystrophic calcifications but can occasionally mimic malignancy.

fields.Text: For NRC medical events, the referring physician and patient must generally be notified within {{c1::24 hours}}.
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: NRC medical event reporting requires telephone notification to NRC Operations Center within the next calendar day, written report to NRC Regional Office within 15 days, and patient/referring physician notification within 24 hours of discovery.




Open review Q47

fields.Text:
Lumbar spine defect through the pars on radiograph/CT. What structure is involved?

{{c1::Pars interarticularis}}
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Spondylolysis is a defect in the pars interarticularis, which is the portion of the neural arch connecting the superior and inferior articular facets. It occurs in approximately 5% of the population and is usually found at the L5 level. CT scan is the preferred imaging technique for adults with MRI recommended as first-line in adolescents.





Open review Q48

fields.Text: Spondylolysis is a defect of the {{c1::pars interarticularis}}.
fields.Extra:

It occurs in approximately 5% of the population and is usually found at the L5 level. CT scan is the preferred imaging technique for adults with MRI recommended as first-line in adolescents.

fields.Text:
Calcaneal expansile lytic lesion with fluid-fluid levels and cortical erosion. Most worrisome diagnosis?

{{c1::Telangiectatic osteosarcoma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q49. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma is a malignant osteogenic tumor. It has a very similar presentation to ABC, which is a non-malignant tumor. Telangiectatic osteosarcoma will have fluid-fluid levels, expansile lesion, and often the cortex is compromised. A histological analysis is needed to differentiate (malignant cells).






Open review Q49

fields.Text: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma can mimic aneurysmal bone cyst with {{c1::fluid-fluid levels}}, but is malignant and more aggressive.
fields.Extra:

Telangiectatic osteosarcoma will have fluid-fluid levels, expansile lesion, and often the cortex is compromised. A histological analysis is needed to differentiate (malignant cells).








Open review Q49

fields.Text:
12-year-old with heterogeneous enhancing renal mass and claw sign. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Renal cell carcinoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q50. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Pediatric RCC is extremely rare and typically occurs in periadolescent children. It appears as a solid mass with hemorrhage, necrosis, calcification, or cystic degeneration on CT. It cannot be distinguished from Wilms tumor on imaging alone. Bilateral RCC or occurrence in younger children suggests von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.





Open review Q50

fields.Text: In a periadolescent child, a heterogeneous solid renal mass with hemorrhage/necrosis/calcification can represent {{c1::renal cell carcinoma}}.

It appears as a solid mass with hemorrhage, necrosis, calcification, or cystic degeneration on CT. It cannot be distinguished from Wilms tumor on imaging alone. Bilateral RCC or occurrence in younger children suggests von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.
